concerns.text: FR-004 mandates a 'two-component Skew-Normal Mixture Model (SNMM)'. The upstream Idea explicitly specifies 'fit a two-component Gaussian mixture model' (Methodology sketch). The Constitution (Principle VI) also mandates 'Gaussian mixture model'. Replacing the specified Gaussian model with a Skew-Normal model redefines the core methodology and the theoretical comparison baseline (which relies on Gaussian assumptions in the cited literature). This is a drift from the research question's defined method.

FR-007 mandates a 'Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)' against Monte Carlo propagated distributions. The upstream Idea explicitly specifies a 'z-test' for comparing the measured slope to theoretical predictions (Methodology sketch). The Constitution (Principle VII) also mandates a 'z-test'. Changing the statistical test from a z-test to an LRT alters the hypothesis testing framework and the definition of the p-value, constituting a drift in the research question's execution.

FR-008 mandates 'Bayesian Change-Point Detection (BCPD)' for validation. The upstream Idea specifies 'kernel density estimation with adaptive bandwidth' as the alternative validation method (Methodology sketch). The Constitution (Principle VI) also mandates 'kernel density estimation'. BCPD is a fundamentally different statistical approach than KDE. This substitution changes the validation logic defined in the idea.

FR-009 mandates a sensitivity analysis using a 'standard two-component Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)'. This implies the primary model (SNMM in FR-004) is NOT Gaussian. However, the Idea and Constitution define the PRIMARY model as Gaussian. This creates a logical contradiction: the spec treats GMM as a sensitivity check for a non-GMM primary, whereas the idea treats GMM as the primary and KDE as the check. This redefines the primary research engine.
